speaker: interviewer
text: Please continue about your little cousin.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. When these striped-uniformed men-- which we didn't know who they were at the time-- took the child from my cousin and gave it to her mother, she protested. But they insisted this is the best way. And this is the last time she saw her child again, or her mother, for that matter. And as we were continuing walking closer to the-- to this place where they-- like I said, it was night, but there were bright lights there. And we walked closer. And all I could say-- see is people being motioned to go one way or the other way. One way or the other way, as, you know, as the lines went on.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when it was our turn, my mother was motioned to go left, and my sister and I were sent right. And all I could see is my mother turning back and saying in German, [CRYING] [SPEAKING GERMAN]. It means, these are also my children.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was shoved. And we were shoved the other way. And that was the last time we saw her. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And after that, we were taken [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to a big hall by this-- of course, the SS soldiers, you know, were beside us and-- and asking us to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember who any of them were?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. But as I was told after, that this was Mengele, who was selecting who goes one way, who goes the other way. Who is capable of working, and who will be going to the gas chambers. But of course, like I said, at that time, we didn't know. All I could see, it's the middle of the night. There was big fires burning, and these uniformed men rushing around, you know, in the striped clothes. And that's all I could see. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And when we got to this hall, it was a big hall, full of soldiers running back and forth, back and forth. And we were all standing there, hundreds and hundreds of women. And we were asked to disrobe. And of course, with all the soldiers around, you know, we were hesitating. And then they started yelling. So we started disrobing. And we were told to take our shoes off, and the shoes we could hold in our hands. And the rest of the clothes, we should just leave them in a pile there. And that's the way we were standing for a while. I can't tell you whether it was an hour or two hours we were standing like that. And like nothing, all those SS soldiers walking back and forth. And still we were standing. And then the next thing was that they were picking out women from the line, the ones with the most beautiful hair first. You know, there was someone had the most beautiful hair-- she would be taken first. And they shaved the hair off. And then the other. But they didn't go in line to say, OK, this line goes, or the other. First, the ones with the most beautiful hair were taken, I guess, just to hurt more. I don't know. So as-- you know, and then, they took everybody. At first, those. After that, we were taken into showers. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we had to wash and shower. And then we got our shoes back, and we were walked out through another door. Not through the door we were first-- we first came in, where we left our clothes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you wearing?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember a plaid dress I was wearing. It had two pockets, and in the pocket, I had my bread. And it was bothering me that the bread for my little brother was left in my pocket of my dress. I won't be able to give him the bread. And of course, I never saw him again. And as we were walking out, there were some mothers and daughters and sisters. One didn't recognize the other. Because from the shaven heads, nobody knew who is who.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the screaming, and the yelling, you know. Where are you? And you know? And here, they could have been standing practically close to each other, or next to each other, but they didn't recognize one another. Did you stay with your sister the whole time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was with my sister the whole time. And then we had to go sort of where they were giving us some dresses. No underwear or anything like that, just dresses. And it so happened-- whether it was intentionally or unintentionally-- like, big people would get a small little dress. A small person would get a huge dress. I was very small and thin at the time, and I got a huge, black dress. To me, it looked like a size 50, you know, I would think, now. A huge, black dress.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I thought, oh, how can I wear such a dress? I took the dress, and I went back to her, while she was sitting there, giving out the dresses. And I says, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. It's too big. She gave me such a slap, and said, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] Paris, which means you're not going to Paris. And of course, I took my dress back, and I went on.

speaker: interviewee
text: It came in very handy, because I tore off a piece from the bottom and made-- made a turban, because we were shaven off, you know, the hair. We weren't allowed to do that. They told us not to cover our hair-- head. But we did it anyway.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I gave a piece to my sister, and she put it on her head. And the next thing, we were taken to a big field, where we were standing. And that's called zelapel. They were counting us. You know, the count? And we were standing there for hours. The sun came up. The sun was burning, you know? So hot. And we were standing in that sun. And people who didn't have covers on their hair, it was terrible. No food.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after a while in the-- about noontime, they were giving us some dishes, some plates. And then it started raining. And we collected the rainwater, sort of, and we were drinking that. And then they came around with some food. I can't remember what it was. And we were standing there some more. And towards evening, they took us into a big hall, like a barrack. An empty barrack. And they told us to lie down. Sleep on the floor. And we did lie down. We were exhausted, like the night before, not sleeping. And the days we were in the cars, we weren't sleeping. And all I could remember that two soldiers came in, and they took a girl out, because she had-- she went into a coma. She was a diabetic, we were told by her relatives after. They just carted her out, and that was it. And we stayed there, and early in the morning, we were woken up and given some coffee, and back outside, standing in the sun. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember approximately how many people you were with in that barrack?

speaker: interviewee
text: In that barrack? Oh, must have been a few hundred. I can't remember. I know we were so closely one to the next, lying there, that it was no room. No room to walk.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then what happened in the morning?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were standing outside again, zelapel, which they called. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] at that time, we didn't know what's going to happen. Towards noon, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were told that anybody who's not 16, you know, is below 16 years of age, should come forward. That they would be taken to a workplace [COUGHS] that is much lighter work than the other ones. Of course, my sister-- I was approaching-- you know, I was 16, and my sister was below that. And we were going to separate. We already hugged and kissed. And the people around her, even my own cousins, said, oh, maybe she should. It's going to be much lighter work for her. It's going to be easier. And as we were hugging and kissing, something came into my mind, like-- like if-- something like an angel would come and say, no, what you could do, she could do. Don't separate. And then I said, no, Ella, you're not going. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And up to this day, she can't forget. She says, you saved my life. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we didn't-- she didn't come forward. Of course, we didn't have names. Nobody called us by name or anything. We were nothing. And nobody knew how old anybody was, so it was whatever we said or whatever we looked like. And she was just as tall as I was at the time, you know, so she didn't really look as a child. And we stayed together. And all that day, we were still outside. And they brought some soup along. And then-- then we were taken back to the same barrack for sleeping at night again. And in the next morning, we were really shipped out of there, like the next day. And we were taken-- there were trains coming right into the camp, and we were just taken to trains. And at that time, we were asked about the names, because they were trying to put the people with the same letters, like with the same alphabet, together. And my sister and I, of course, we were Drummer. And my cousin, she was married to a Drummer, so she was going to stay with us. But she had her sister there, who was a [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. And she says, you know, I'll give my name as [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. I want to stay with her, I don't know what's going to happen, which was the right thing. So we were put in one of those cars, and she went in another car. And the next thing, we moved, like we started traveling. And where we came to, our cars were taken off in Latvia. And my cousin and her sister, they continued, which we found out after they went to-- to Estonia. They were taken to Estonia. And us in Latvia were taken to the camp there, to Riga.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your cousin survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And her sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long was your train ride to Riga?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not long. It-- it-- I don't think-- think that it was a day. It wasn't long. But you know, we also stopped, like I said, when they were-- because some-- some of them were taken off at Lithuania. And then it was  Latvia. And then it was Estonia.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened? What did you see when the doors to the train opened?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were rushed out, to rush out. And we were taken by trucks to this camp, which we find out it's called Riga-Kaiserwald. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were-- during the day-- we were there for about three weeks-- during the day, we were taken to the railroad and sort of picking stones and carting stones in the-- in the wheelbarrows. That's what I remember. And then at night, they would bring us back to the camp. And that was going on for about three weeks. It wasn't as large camp as Auschwitz, or anything like that. But of course, nobody-- everybody was busy looking after themselves. It wasn't much that we thought. As a matter of fact, we couldn't think. Because I think we were stopped and given drugs in our food, because nobody was thinking of anything. And when we were talking to one another, it was always, oh, when we'll be free, and we'll go home to our homes and to our parents. None of us, none of us, believed that this is not going to be that way. That our parents were killed. None of us were thinking of that. And because we were so hungry, we would sit and-- and sort of talk about cooking recipes. How good this was, or how good that would be. And I did it-- my parents did it this way, or my mother cooked it that way. This is what we were talking about.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were all the girls about the same age?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not necessarily. Because there were-- like I said, there were some mothers and daughters, too, you know. Younger. And I suppose my mother could have come with us, because she was young. But because she had a child and another child with her, she was taken. Yeah. So there were anywhere from 14 to 40.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how many people there were, maybe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were a lot in the camp. But different people, what they can do different work. And that-- and-- and that was the first time we worked, was in Auchswitz. We didn't work while in the ghetto. We didn't work. And after about three weeks, we were shipped out.

speaker: interviewer
text: How?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we were shipped out-- it was by train-- to an area where it was forests. And we were-- there were tents set up. Black tents. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And for bathrooms, there were latrines, you know, just huge latrines with bars. Those were the-- and there was-- it was daylight 24 hours a day. The sun would go down, but there it wasn't night. And in the mornings, sometimes you would get up, and there was, like, dew. Wet. Everything was wet. And we wouldn't-- you know, that one could hide in the-- on the fields and not be seen, because the-- You know, it-- later-- I didn't know at the time-- we were close to the-- by the Baltic Sea.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in the daytime, we used to work in the forest, cutting trees. Could you imagine? Girls cutting trees with saws. And then after the trees would fall, we had to cut them into pieces. And then taken back to camp at night, like which was the-- you know, bed.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's where we get our food, the little food what we'd get. We'd get-- like, we'd get a bread, and that bread would be divided into-- the bread was as big as a brick, and it was the shape of a brick. And that was to be divided for five. That was the daily ration of bread. And we would get the black coffee in the morning. And at night, we'd get a soup. And that was the food. A thick soup. There was some meat in it. But at first, we couldn't eat it, because it was smelly. But after a while, we could eat it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And some people used to get the bread, and they would eat it right away, and they'd have nothing all day. But my sister and I, we always saved. We always saved a piece for later, for other times. Came in handy. And then we were there till August that year, 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the town where the camp was?

speaker: interviewee
text: The name of the town was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] oh, I have to-- it was [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. Yeah. It was-- like I said, it was in the-- we didn't see a town. All we could see is the forest and this empty space where we were staying.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did the camp have a name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, just-- because I remember, we were making up songs about it, you know, at night, after we came back. We would sit around and make up songs about the camp. It was [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], and I forget now what the words were like.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then in August, what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then in August-- and, like, I'm not sure of the date. In August, all of a sudden we were taken to this lake, or water, or whatever. You know. We were walking. And there, with little boats-- not big ones-- we were taken across.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you walking for?

speaker: interviewee
text: We must have been walking a couple of days.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was there? Who were the soldiers? What nationality?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were Latvians. And they were, believe me, worse than the German SS. They were Latvian SS. But they were so bad. So bad. So-- beating. Oh, they would beat for every little thing. And they were like-- like mad dogs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you or your sister ever get beaten?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did, but later on, yes. Not there, not at that camp. Not my sister. I did. And then [INAUDIBLE] I remember sitting on the shore and waiting for the turn, so when they'll take us across. And we were not told, but somehow, some people find out that the reason we are taken from there is because the Russian army is approaching. Like, Russians are approaching, therefore they took us out. And after we crossed that with the boat, with those boats, we were standing on the other side of the shore, waiting till everybody came. And then we were taken to a camp, a concentration camp by the name of Stutthof. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And in Stutthof, we were-- we were there for three weeks in Stutthof. About three-- two or three weeks. I'm not sure of the time. All I remember is we were then put into one of those barracks. We were given a-- well, by the way, we were given a number in Riga, also.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Go back to that. Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I have to look that up, the number, because I don't remember it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that number was taken away as we came back-- as we came to Stutthof. And in Stutthof, we were without a number. And we were put into this barrack, and every day, there were selections. Like they'd say, you'd have to come out to zelapel, you know? Hundreds and hundreds of us. And there would be a committee, like of people who would look everyone over, and they would select for work. And the ones they select, they would tell them to go, come out of the line, and go to another place. And my sister and I were never selected, because we were so run-down by then. We were not good for work anymore. So the ones which were not selected, in Stutthof, there was like an infirmary. And Stutthof also had a lot of gypsies inmates. And the gypsies were sort of even taking care of us, you know, like we were below. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So people who were not selected for work, they would take them over to the infirmary, which the infirmary was a big wire fence, a high wire fence with a big gate of wire, like a wire fence gate. And a gypsy would stand at the gates so no one could go in, no one could go out, without their allowing that. And I remember, my sister and I were sent there. And by that time, we knew, you're never allowed to say you're sick. You're never allowed to say you're weak. You're always strong, and you could always manage. And we saw ourselves in the infirmary there, with sick people who were in their bunks. And every morning-- like, we stayed there an overnight-- the next morning, they would take all the dead and the sick, and they would take them on carts and take them out to the crematorium. Of course--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the crematorium at Stutthof?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Stutthof, there was one crematorium. Yes. And because there was only one crematorium, they couldn't burn so many at once. So first, they would take the dead, and then the sick. And then, you know, they had a quota. So many. They would take the others who were there. Therefore, it was a dangerous place to be.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a courtyard. You know, we came out the next morning, after they took all the sick, and there were some of us left. We walked out into the courtyard, my sister and I. And we saw at the gate that the girl comes in with a pail of disinfectant, you know? And she's carrying the full pail of disinfectant, this one girl. And she empties it, and the gypsy at the door lets her out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when the next one came, my-- I-- and she was going back out, I helped her carry the pail out, instead of her being alone. She carried it in alone. It was a Jewish girl. I helped her carry it out. And the gypsy, she probably saw. And she probably knew what we are doing, but she let us go through. And my sister did the same. So we came through to the other side of the camp, which was OK already. And we went into whatever barrack there was. We just went in, and we would lie down. And when they were distributing food, we just went and got some food for us. But we were not accounted for anymore, because we were already, as-- as-- you know, sent to the infirmary. That means that we were-- they were finished with us. But we came back.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there we stayed. And then when there was a next selection, we stood in line again to be selected. It can be worse. And then the guy remembered that he took us, because he looked at us. And he was shaking his head, thinking, how did we come back?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, we were taken back to the infirmary. And a day later, we did the same thing. We walked back out, with the help of the holding the pail, and the girl letting us through to the other side. And then we went into a barrack, where we met some women who were Polish Jews, who were in camps already for years. And they knew the ropes. And they were-- and not so much me, because I was older, but my sister was still so young. And she sort of felt so sorry for my sister, one of those ladies. And she says, you know what, there is going to be a selection tonight. It's going to be dark. And I'm going out with this transport also, because she is going to be sort of the manager of the kitchen there. And I'll make sure that you're in it. And we went out, and it was the evening already. And we were selected into this transport. And when we got out-- and when that was-- we were very happy to get out of Stutthof alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you trust-- why did you trust her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because she was a Jewish woman who-- who was there in camp for so long. And you could see she was kind. And she-- and she felt sorry for us, because we told her what happened the previous couple of times. And we were selected into that group. And there were 1,500 selected into that group. And the next day-- and then we were already separated from the rest of the camp, you know, all this group of people. So nobody could come in or go out from this group. And we were sent out the next morning. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But before that-- I should really go back, which I forgot to mention. That when we first arrived to Stutthof, the first selection, when we were not taken, we hadn't-- they didn't send us to the infirmary yet, because that selection, they were very selective who they are taking. So they didn't care so much, you know, that they're leaving so many behind. And our friends who we were with in Latvia at the time came from our country, and we became very good friends. They were selected to go, and they were two sisters. And we wanted to go so much with them, and we couldn't. They wouldn't let us. And there were the SS already guarding them, that nobody else should get into their group. And I-- I, my sister, somehow or other, we managed to push into that group. And we were standing with them, just before they were leaving, and they counted that there were two more. And they counted again, and there were two more. And then somehow, whether they noticed, or somebody told, they grabbed me out of line. The SS, it was a woman SS. She beat the daylight out of me. Not my sister, me. She beat me so hard that my sister thought that this is the end, that I'll never be able to get up. But I did get up.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm going to stop the tape there, and we'll continue.
